en: From your letter of 16th February I want first to single out the end, where you so admirably assign to its proper place the power of suggestion in psychoanalysis: "The patient is no empty sack, into which you can cram what you will; he brings his own predetermined content with him, with which one has always to reckon afresh." With this I fully agree, my own experience confirms it. And you add: "This content remains untouched by involuntary analytical suggestion, but its form is altered, proteus-fashion, beyond measure." So it becomes a matter of a sort of "mimicry" by which the patient seeks to escape the analyst, who is driving him into a corner and therefore for the moment seems to him an enemy. Until at last, through the joint work of patient and analyst—the former spontaneously yielding up his psychic content, the latter only interpreting and explaining—the analysis succeeds in bringing so much light into the darkness of the patient's psyche that he can see the true relationships and, without any preconceived plan of the analyst's, can himself draw the right conclusions and apply them to his future life. This new life will betake itself along the line of least resistance—or should we not rather say, the least resistances, as a "compromise with all the necessities," in a just balancing of pleasure and unpleasure? It is not we who must arbitrarily seek to determine how matters stand for the patient and what will benefit him; his own nature decides. In other words, we must assume the rôle of the accoucheur who can bring out into the light of day a child already alive, but who must avoid a series of mistakes if the child is to remain able to live and the mother is not to be injured. All this is very clear to me, since it is only the application to the psychoanalytic method of a general principle which should have universal validity: never do violence to Nature. Hence I also see that the psychoanalyst must follow his patient's apparently "wrong roads" if the patient is ever to arrive at his own convictions and be freed once and for all from infantile reliance on authority. We ourselves as individuals have learnt or can only learn by making mistakes how to avoid them for the future, and mankind as a whole has created the conditions of its present and future stages of development quite as much by frequent travel along wrong paths as along the right road. Have not many neurotics—I do not know if you will agree, but I think so—become ill partly for the very reason that their infantile faith in authority has fallen to pieces? Now they stand before the wreckage of their faith, weeping over it, in dire distress because they cannot find a substitute which shall show them clearly whither their life's course should now turn. So they remain stuck fast betwixt infancy which they must unwillingly renounce, and the serious duties of the present and future (the moral conflict). I see, particularly in such cases, you are right in saying it is a mistake to seek to replace the lost faith in authority by another similar faith, certain to be useful only so long as the belief lasts. This applies to the deliberate use of suggestion in psychoanalysis, and the building upon the transference to the doctor as the object of the analytic therapy. I am no longer in doubt about your maxim: "Every interference on the analyst's part is a gross mistake in technique. So-called chance is the law and the order of psychoanalysis." Further, I am entirely in agreement with you when you say that altruism necessarily must be innate in man considered as a herd-animal . The contrary would be the thing to be wondered at.
zh: 從您二月十六日的來信，我首先想特別指出結尾的部分，您在其中極其出色地將精神分析中暗示的力量歸於其應有的位置：「病人並非一個空麻袋，您可以隨意向其中塞入任何東西；他隨身攜帶自己預先決定的內容，我們必須時時重新與之計量。」對此我完全同意，我自身的經驗也證實了這一點。您又補充道：「這個內容不受非自願的分析性暗示所觸動，但其形式卻像普羅透斯一般，被改變得無以復加。」因此，這就成了一種某種「模仿」的問題，病人藉此試圖逃脫那位將他逼入死角、因而此刻在他看來如同敵人的分析師。直到最後，透過病人與分析師的共同工作——前者自發地交出他的心靈內容，後者僅作詮釋與說明——分析成功地將如此多的光照進病人心靈的黑暗之中，以致他能看清真實的關係，並且在沒有任何分析師預設計畫的情況下，自行得出正確的結論，並將其應用於未來的生活。這新生命將沿著阻力最小的路線前行——或者我們是否更應該說，是「與所有必然性妥協」下的最小阻力路線，在快樂與不快樂之間取得公正的平衡？並非我們必須任意地試圖決定病人的狀況如何、什麼對他有益；是他自己的本性來決定。換句話說，我們必須扮演助產士的角色，他能將一個已然存活的嬰兒帶到日光之下，但若要使嬰兒能繼續存活、母親不受傷害，就必須避免一系列錯誤。這一切對我而言非常清楚，因為這不過是將一條應具普遍效力的總原則應用於精神分析方法：絕不對自然施暴。因此，我也明白精神分析師必須跟隨病人表面上「錯誤的道路」，如果病人要最終達到他自己的確信，並一勞永逸地脫離對權威的嬰兒式依賴。我們作為個體，過去或只能透過犯錯來學習如何在未來避免它們；而人類作為整體，創造其現在與未來發展階段的條件，同樣是藉由頻繁地行走在錯誤的道路上，正如行走在正確的道路上一樣。許多神經症患者——我不知道您是否同意，但我認為如此——難道不正是部分因為他們對權威的嬰兒式信仰已經破碎而致病的嗎？如今他們站在自己信仰的廢墟前，為之哭泣，處於極度的痛苦之中，因為他們找不到一個替代品，能清楚地向他們指明他們的人生道路此刻應轉向何方。因此，他們被困在必須不情願地放棄的嬰兒期，與現在和未來的嚴肅責任（道德衝突）之間動彈不得。我特別在這樣的案例中看到，您說試圖用另一種相似的信仰來取代已失去的對權威的信仰是錯誤的，這話是對的；這種信仰只有在信仰持續期間才確定有用。這適用於精神分析中對暗示的刻意使用，以及建立在將對醫生的移情作為分析治療對象之上的做法。我對您的格言不再有任何懷疑：「分析師方面的任何干預，都是技術上的重大錯誤。所謂的機遇，就是精神分析的法則與秩序。」此外，我完全同意您所說的，利他主義必然是人類作為群居動物與生俱來的。相反的情況才令人費解。
